context: the Bible tells us at the end of chapter 33 in the book of Ezekiel about the fact that they heard the words, but they didn't do it.
point_summary: Ezekiel 33 is cited as a parallel witness to Jeremiah's call to repentance, noting that Israel heard the prophetic word but failed to obey it.

context: God tells Ezekiel that if you warn, then you're free. You don't have any blood on your hands.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am pure from the blood of all men" in Acts 20 consciously echoes Ezekiel's watchman principle: faithful warning discharges the messenger's responsibility regardless of how hearers respond.

context: If you fail to warn, the blood's on your hands.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am pure from the blood of all men" in Acts 20 deliberately echoes Ezekiel's watchman principle: the one who faithfully warns bears no guilt for those who refuse to heed.

context: But I will say this, it's of note that in Ezekiel chapter 48, in verses 1 and
point_summary: Dan's absence from Revelation 7 and 1 Chronicles 2–10 is contrasted with his inclusion in Ezekiel 48:1–2, which is read as a millennial restoration indicating God's long-range purposes extend beyond tribal judgment.

context: ...in Ezekiel 48, verses 1 and 2, in fact, you can look at Ezekiel...

context: Here's what God says to Ezekiel. I've called you to be a watchman. You can read it in chapter three...
point_summary: God calls his messengers as watchmen who must sound the warning regardless of response, with the responsibility for heeding or rejecting that warning falling entirely on the hearers.

context: and then again in chapter 33, and God says to Ezekiel that you give them warning...
point_summary: God's call to Ezekiel as a watchman establishes that the messenger's responsibility is fulfilled in delivering the warning; how hearers respond falls entirely on them.

context: There was a great indictment that was brought in the days of Ezekiel. In fact, turn there, if you will, and read with me Ezekiel chapter 22.
point_summary: God's word, not human opinion, defines evil — a point illustrated by the sweeping indictment of Israel's violations catalogued in Ezekiel 22 as a scriptural case study in what evil actually looks like.

context: The Bible says this, her priests have violated my law. Notice what it says, and have profaned mine holy things. They have put no difference between the holy and profane, neither have they showed difference between the unclean and the clean, and have hid their eyes from my Sabbaths, and I am profaned among them.
point_summary: Ezekiel 22:26 is cited as a sobering indictment showing that when priests failed to distinguish between holy and profane, clean and unclean, they profaned God himself.

context: And we're familiar with verse number 30 when the Bible says that God sought for a man, but he found none.
point_summary: God's definition of evil is illustrated in Ezekiel 22 by priests who erased the distinction between holy and profane, clean and unclean—a failure to differentiate that Scripture itself treats as moral violation.

context: I believe that in verse number 26 that Paul is making a direct, I mean, straight line back to Ezekiel's book when God says that he sent Ezekiel to be a watchman.
point_summary: Paul's declaration in Acts 20:26 that he is "pure from the blood of all men" draws a direct line to Ezekiel's watchman passages (chapters 3 and 33), where faithfulness to warn determines guilt or innocence for others' spiritual fate.

context: Ezekiel 3 and 33, you find that God makes that declaration.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of being "pure from the blood of all men" (Acts 20:26) is read as a direct echo of Ezekiel 3, where God charges the watchman with guilt if he fails to warn the people.

context: Ezekiel 3 and 33, you find that God makes that declaration.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of being "pure from the blood of all men" (Acts 20:26) is read as a direct echo of Ezekiel's watchman commission, where faithfulness to warn absolves the prophet of guilt for those who reject the message.

context: And according to the book of Ezekiel, the watchman doesn't give an account for whether the people respond to the warning. He gives an account on whether he gave the warning.
point_summary: The watchman's accountability before God is for faithfulness in delivering the warning, not for whether those warned respond to it.

context: Ezekiel says that God said, I sought for a man among them that would make up a hedge, that would stand in the gap. But he says, I found none.
point_summary: God's call for one person to "stand in the gap" (Ezek. 22:30) frames Joseph as the faithful individual God used when no such man could be found in Ezekiel's day.

context: You think about the book of Ezekiel, God gives this indictment that God sought for a man among them that should stand in the gap to make up and hedge, but he found, the Bible says he found none.
point_summary: God's failure to find even one man willing to stand in the gap (Ezekiel) stands as a solemn contrast to the faithful 300 in Judges 7 who each stood every man in his place.

context: go back to the book of Ezekiel and read v. 3
point_summary: Ezekiel 3's watchman passage underlies Paul's declaration in Acts 20:26 — faithfully warning others clears the watchman of bloodguilt, while silence makes him accountable for their souls.

context: go back to the book of Ezekiel and read v. 33
point_summary: Ezekiel 33's watchman passage underlies Paul's declaration in Acts 20:26, showing that faithful warning clears the messenger of bloodguilt while silence makes him accountable for those left unwarned.

context: And then in chapter number 40 of the book of Ezekiel, through the end of the book, chapter number 48, you deal with millennial prophecies with regard to Israel.

context: Look in verse number one of Ezekiel 38. The Bible says this, and the word of the Lord came unto me saying, son of man, that's the name, the term that is given to Ezekiel.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:1 opens a prophecy against Gog of Magog that, like fulfilled first-coming prophecies, will come to pass exactly as God declared.

context: the land of Magog, the chief prince of Meshach and Tubal, and prophesy against him.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:1 introduces the prophecy of Gog—identified as a ruler, not a place—whose land Magog is historically and theologically understood to represent Russia.

context: And say, thus saith the Lord God, behold, I am against thee, O Gog, the chief prince of Meshach and Tubal.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:2 identifies "Gog" as a person—a ruling prince—and "Magog" as his land, widely identified by historians and theologians alike as Russia.

context: and I will turn thee back and put hooks into thy jaws, and I will bring thee forth and all thine army, horses and horsemen, and all them, clothed with all sorts of armor, even a great company with bucklers and shields, all of them handling swords,

context: Persia, Ethiopia, and Libya, with them, all of them with shield and helmet,
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:5 lists Persia, Ethiopia, and Libya as nations among the coalition armies gathered under Gog, identifying the specific allied "players" in this end-times prophecy.

context: Gomer and all his bands, the house of Togarmah of the north quarters, and all his bands, and many people with thee.

context: the chief prince of Meshach and Tubal in verse number two of Ezekiel 38. So we find that Gog being the ruler of the people, and Magog that is mentioned there in verse number two.

context: it's interesting, for it tells us in verse number six, notice what it says, of the house of Togarmah, of the north quarters.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:6's reference to "the north quarters," confirmed by verse 15's "north parts," is identified geographically as pointing to Russia, situated nearly due north of Jerusalem.

context: Skip down, if you will, to verse number 15, and notice what the Bible says there. And thou shalt come from thy place, notice what it says, out of the north parts, thou and many people with thee, all of them riding upon horses, a great company, and a mighty army.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:15's reference to an army coming "out of the north parts" is read alongside verse 6's "north quarters" to identify Russia geographically as the invading force, with Moscow cited as nearly due north of Jerusalem.

context: Notice he speaks also of Ethiopia in verse number five and Libya with them
point_summary: Persia, named in Ezekiel 38:5, is identified as the modern nation of Iran, a connection grounded in the historical renaming from Persia to Iran in 1935.

context: in verse number six, he speaks of Gomer
point_summary: Gomer, listed in verse 6, is identified with the modern regions of Turkey and Ukraine, placing them among the coalition of nations prophesied to move against Israel.

point_summary: Gomer, listed in verse 6, is identified with the modern regions of Turkey and Ukraine, placing them among the coalition of nations prophesied to move against Israel.

context: But I want you to understand something very important in verse number four. God is speaking
point_summary: God's sovereign orchestration of the Gog coalition is seen in the "hooks into thy jaws" language of verse 4, where He compels the advance of enemy nations despite their own independent evil intent.

context: He says in verse number three, thus saith the Lord God
point_summary: God's sovereign orchestration of the Gog coalition is underscored by the divine "I will" of verse 4, establishing that even enemy nations with their own evil intent act ultimately under God's control.

context: Be thou prepared, and prepare for thyself, thou, and all thy company, that are assembled unto thee, and be thou a guard unto them.
point_summary: God's sovereign control over the Gog coalition is underscored by Ezekiel 38:7's command to "be thou prepared," which frames the nations' evil intent as ultimately subject to divine orchestration rather than their own autonomous will.

context: in the latter years thou shalt come into the land that is brought back from the sword
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:8's phrase "in the latter years" is cited as a temporal marker identifying the Gog and Magog invasion as an end-times event, with current events viewed as potentially leading toward its fulfillment.

context: And thou shalt come up against my people of Israel as a cloud to cover the land
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:16's phrase "latter days" corroborates verse 8's "latter years," together establishing the eschatological timing of the Gog and Magog invasion against Israel.

context: up to the land of unwalled villages, I will go with them that, notice what it says, that are at rest, that dwell safely, all of them dwelling without walls and having neither bars nor gates.
point_summary: The description of Israel dwelling in unwalled villages, at rest and dwelling safely, signals a future prophetic condition not yet present in Israel's current state of heightened military readiness.

context: It said at the end of verse number eight, and they shall dwell safely, all of them.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:8's reference to Israel dwelling safely in "unwalled villages" points to a future prophetic fulfillment distinct from Israel's current state of heightened military vigilance.

context: therefore, son of man, prophesy and say unto God, thus saith the Lord God. And that day when my people of Israel dwelleth safely, shalt thou not know it.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:14's reference to Israel dwelling safely signals that this invasion cannot occur under present conditions, pointing to a future prophetic fulfillment yet to come.

context: the players that are mentioned in the first six verses of Ezekiel 38
point_summary: Ezekiel 38's opening verses identify the players and timing of the Gog-Magog invasion as falling within the tribulation period, when Israel's perceived safety under the antichrist's peace treaty prevails.

context: In verse number 12, we noted about the spoil that they would take a prey to turn thine hand upon the desolate places that are now inhabited, upon the people that are gathered out of the nations.
point_summary: Ezekiel 38:12 identifies the motivation for Gog's invasion as economic plunder—silver, gold, cattle, and goods—from a regathered, prosperous Israel dwelling in perceived safety.

context: thus saith the Lord God, in that day when my people of Israel dwell safely that thou shalt thou not know it.
point_summary: The literal mention of horses in Ezekiel's Gog and Magog prophecy is defended as genuinely literal, not merely figurative for modern weaponry, with the tribulation period's conditions making horse-based warfare plausible.

context: And thou shalt come from thy place out of the north parts now, and many people with thee, all of them riding upon horses, a great company and a mighty army.
point_summary: The literal reference to a great army coming "from the north parts" on horses is defended as genuine prophetic description, not primitive metaphor, with the observation that modern nations like Russia and China maintain millions of horses.

context: Notice in verse number 16 of Ezekiel 38, it says, and thou shalt come up against my people of Israel as a cloud to cover the land. It shall be in the latter days.
point_summary: God's purpose in bringing Gog against Israel in the latter days is not geopolitical accident but divine intervention, so that the heathen nations may know and recognize Him.

context: and I will smite thy bow out of thy left hand and will cause thine arrows to fall out of thy right hand.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Thou shalt fall upon the mountains of Israel, thou and all thy bands and the people that is with thee, I will give thee unto the ravenous birds of every sort and to the beast of the field to be devoured.
point_summary: God's judgment on Gog results in such total military defeat that Israel's slain enemies and abandoned weapons will take seven months to bury and seven years to burn.

context: and they that dwell in the cities of Israel shall go forth and shall set on fire and burn the weapons,
point_summary: The completeness of God's victory over Gog is underscored by the detail that Israel will spend seven years burning the captured weapons, requiring no other fuel.

context: ...they shall burn them with fire seven years...
point_summary: The seven-year duration of weapon-burning following God's defeat of Gog distinguishes Ezekiel's Gog-Magog battle from the post-millennial Gog-Magog of Revelation 20, establishing them as separate prophetic events.

context: ...so that they shall take no wood out of the field...
point_summary: Israel's victory over the Gog-Magog coalition will be so total that the weapons alone will fuel seven years of burning, distinguishing this battle from the separate Gog-Magog event at the end of the millennium in Revelation 20.

context: ...in seven months shall the house of Israel be bearing of them...
point_summary: The completeness of God's victory over Gog is measured by the seven months Israel must spend burying the dead to cleanse the land, distinguishing this Ezekiel battle from the separate Gog and Magog event in Revelation 20.

context: Ezekiel's prophecy is not the same that John writes about in Revelation chapter number 20.
point_summary: The Gog and Magog invasion of Ezekiel 38–39 is distinguished from the Gog and Magog of Revelation 20 by its aftermath: seven years of burning weapons and seven months of burying the dead, details incompatible with the post-millennial context.

context: Ezekiel's prophecy is not the same that John writes about in Revelation chapter number 20.
point_summary: The seven-year weapon-burning and seven-month burial described in Ezekiel 39 distinguish its Gog and Magog conflict from the one in Revelation 20, placing them as separate prophetic events with different timings and scales.

context: it shall be in the latter days, pick it up right in the middle of the verse, and I will bring thee against my land. Notice what it says, that the heathen may know me.
point_summary: The Gog-Magog invasion is presented as divinely orchestrated so that Gentile nations will know God, with Ezekiel 38:16 cited as explicit textual evidence of that stated divine purpose.

context: thus will I magnify myself and sanctify myself, and I will be known in the eyes of many nations, and they shall know that I am the Lord.
point_summary: God's ultimate purpose in the Gog-Magog prophecy is self-revelation: that he will magnify and sanctify himself so that many nations know he is the Lord.

context: and I will send a fire on Magog.
point_summary: God's judgment on Magog by fire serves the purpose of making himself known to the Gentile nations, as the text declares, "they shall know that I am the Lord."

context: And notice what it says, and they shall know that I am the Lord. Notice one more time, in verse number 21 of Ezekiel 39, it says, and I will set my glory among the heathen, and all the heathen shall see my judgment that I have executed, and my hand that I have laid upon them.
point_summary: God's judgment on the nations in the Gog-Magog conflict serves the purpose of setting His glory among the Gentiles, making Himself universally known through executed judgment.

context: so will I make my holy name known in the midst of my people Israel, and I will not let them pollute my holy name anymore. And the heathen shall know that I am the Lord, the Holy One in Israel.
point_summary: God's revelation of His holy name in the midst of Israel during the tribulation period serves as His means of making Himself known to His people as their Messiah, whom they had previously rejected.

context: so the house of Israel shall know that I am the Lord their God, notice this phrase, from that day and forward.
point_summary: The tribulation period will serve as God's decisive self-revelation to Israel, so that the house of Israel shall know him as their Lord and God from that day forward.

context: and the heathen shall know that the house of Israel went into captivity, why, for their iniquity, because they trespassed against me, therefore hid I my face from them, and gave them into the hand of their enemies, so fell they all by the sword, according to their uncleanness, and according to their transgressions, have I done unto them, and hid my face from them,
point_summary: Ezekiel 39:23 reveals that Israel's past captivity and God's hidden face were direct consequences of their iniquity and trespass, setting the stage for his future mercy and restoration.

context: therefore thus saith the Lord God, now will I bring again the captivity of Jacob, and have mercy upon the whole house of Israel, and will be jealous for my holy name
point_summary: God's restoration of Israel and outpouring of His Spirit upon the house of Israel serve ultimately to make Him known both to the nations and to Israel herself, not merely to satisfy prophetic curiosity.

context: after that they have borne their shame, and all their trespasses, whereby they have trespassed against me, when they dwelt safely in their land, and none made them afraid

context: when I have brought them again from the people, and gathered them out of their enemies' lands, and am sanctified in them in the sight of many nations
point_summary: God's regathering of Israel from among the nations serves the ultimate purpose of making himself known—both to the heathen and to the house of Israel—not merely as prophetic curiosity but as divine self-revelation.

context: Then shall they know that I am the Lord their God, which caused them to be led into captivity among the heathen, but I have gathered them unto their own land, and have left none of them anymore there
point_summary: God's regathering of Israel and outpouring of His Spirit serve ultimately to make Him known both to the nations and to the house of Israel, not merely to satisfy prophetic curiosity.

context: neither will I hide my face anymore from them, for I have poured out my spirit upon the house of Israel, saith the Lord God
point_summary: God's outpouring of His Spirit upon the house of Israel is presented as the culminating act by which He makes Himself known to both Israel and the nations, fulfilling His redemptive purpose for His people.

context: What's God's purpose in Ezekiel 38 and 39 through all of this situation? To make himself known.
point_summary: God's purpose in the Ezekiel 38–39 events — making Himself known to both the heathen and the house of Israel — is held up as the pattern and motivation for the church's present calling to make Christ known.

context: What's God's purpose in Ezekiel 38 and 39 through all of this situation? To make himself known.
point_summary: God's ultimate purpose in the Ezekiel 38–39 judgment and restoration is to make Himself known—to the nations and to Israel—and that same purpose now drives the church's mission to make Christ known.

point_summary: God's appointment of Ezekiel as a watchman obligated to sound warning—on pain of bloodguilt for silence—grounds the believer's urgent responsibility to proclaim the gospel before the day approaches.

context: You know, it's interesting that on two occasions, in Ezekiel 3 and again in Ezekiel 33, you know what God tells Ezekiel? He set him to be a watchman.
point_summary: God's appointment of Ezekiel as watchman in Ezekiel 33 illustrates the believer's solemn responsibility to sound a gospel warning, with failure to do so bringing accountability for the lost.

point_summary: God's appointment of Ezekiel as watchman—obligating him to warn or bear blood-guilt—is presented as the Old Testament backdrop Paul consciously echoed when declaring himself "free from the blood of all men" in Acts 20.

context: he absolutely had Ezekiel 3 and 33 on his mind
point_summary: God's appointment of Ezekiel as watchman—obligating him to warn or bear blood-guilt—is presented as the Old Testament backdrop Paul consciously drew on when declaring himself "free from the blood of all men" in Acts 20.

point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as I live, saith the Lord God, surely with a mighty hand and with stretched out arm and with fury poured out, will I rule over you
point_summary: God's future regathering of scattered Israel will include divine judgment and pleading—not automatic salvation—demonstrating that "all Israel shall be saved" requires a sovereign purging work before covenant restoration.

reference_text: stretched out arm and with fury poured out
context: stretched out arm and with fury poured out
point_summary: Ezekiel 20:33–40 is cited to show that God's future regathering of Israel includes purging rebels and bringing the nation into covenant bond—demonstrating that ethnic descent alone does not guarantee salvation.

reference_text: I will bring you into the wilderness of the people
context: I will bring you into the wilderness of the people and there will I plead with you face to face
point_summary: God's future gathering of Israel involves a purging judgment—separating rebels from the covenant community—so that "all Israel shall be saved" refers not to ethnic universalism but to a refined remnant prepared to receive Messiah.

reference_text: like as I pleaded with your fathers in the wilderness of the land of Egypt
context: like as I pleaded with your fathers in the wilderness of the land of Egypt
point_summary: God's future gathering of Israel involves a purging judgment—separating rebels from the covenant community—so that "all Israel shall be saved" refers not to ethnic universalism but to a refined remnant brought into covenant relationship with Messiah.

reference_text: I will cause you to pass under the rod and I will bring you into the bond of the covenant
context: I will cause you to pass under the rod and I will bring you into the bond of the covenant
point_summary: God's future purging of rebels from Israel (v. 37) demonstrates that "all Israel shall be saved" cannot mean every ethnic Jew is saved, since unrepentant transgressors are explicitly excluded from entering the land.

reference_text: I will purge out from among you the rebels and them that transgress against me
context: I will purge out from among you the rebels and them that transgress against me
point_summary: God's purging of rebels from among Israel (v. 38) demonstrates that "all Israel shall be saved" does not mean every ethnic Jew, but only those brought into covenant after the unfaithful are removed.

context: as for you, O house of Israel, thus saith the Lord God, go ye, serve ye everyone his idols
point_summary: Ezekiel 20:39–41 shows that "all the house of Israel" serving God occurs only after a future purging of rebels, refuting any claim that ethnic Israel is presently or automatically saved.

reference_text: For in mine holy mountain, in the mountain of the height of Israel
context: For in mine holy mountain, in the mountain of the height of Israel, saith the Lord God
point_summary: After God purges the rebels from Israel and gathers the remnant, all the house of Israel will serve Him in the holy mountain—demonstrating that "all Israel shall be saved" refers to a purified, covenant remnant, not every ethnic Jew.

context: there shall all the house of Israel, all of them in the land serve me. There will I accept them and there will I require your offerings and the first fruits of your oblations with all your holy things.
point_summary: On the holy mountain of Israel, all the house of Israel will serve God and be accepted by Him — a future national restoration that the sermon connects to the partial, temporary blindness of Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in.

context: I will accept you with your sweet savor when I bring you out from the people and gather you out of the countries wherein you have been scattered and I will be sanctified in you before the heathen.
point_summary: God's future regathering and acceptance of Israel — receiving their offerings and being sanctified among the nations — serves as the Ezekiel backdrop for understanding the partial, temporary blindness of Israel in Romans 11.

context: And ye shall know that I am the Lord when I shall bring you into the land of Israel, into the country for the which I lifted up mine hand to give it to your fathers.
point_summary: Israel's future restoration to the land is tied to self-loathing repentance and the repeated divine declaration "ye shall know that I am the Lord," framing the Ezekiel passage as prophecy of God preparing Israel to receive Messiah.

context: And there shall you remember your ways and all your doings wherein you have been defiled and you shall loathe yourselves in your own sight for all your evils that you have committed.
point_summary: Israel's future self-loathing and recognition of God's name-driven restoration, not their merit, illustrates the national repentance that prepares Israel to receive Messiah.

context: you go back to the book of Ezekiel and you look at the son of man, Ezekiel, as he's called, referred to many times in that book, that he was set as being a watchman and he's given this responsibility, you read it in chapter three
point_summary: Ezekiel's watchman commission (ch. 3 and 33) grounds Paul's declaration "your blood be upon your own heads, I am clean"—faithfulness in warning transfers accountability to the hearers.

context: you read it in chapter three and also in chapter 33 and you read in the text of scripture that he's given the responsibility to sound the alarm
point_summary: Paul's declaration that "your blood be upon your own heads, I am clean" echoes Ezekiel's watchman principle: faithful proclamation transfers moral accountability for rejection from the messenger to the hearers.
